coredata.dc:title: Amino benzene dicarboxylic acid-derived luminescent nitrogen-doped Carbon- quantum Dots/anti-TNT antibodies conjugate for detection of nitroaromatic contaminant in water: A comparative analysis of chemo-Bio-sensing affinity
coredata.dc:creator: Devi, Sarita
Garg, Vaishali
Tyagi, Sachin
coredata.dc:description: Water contaminants are posing deadly impacts on living beings' survival. Nitroaromatic compounds e.g. Trinitrotoluene (TNT), are one among these lethal contaminants and require to be early detected before their consumption. The nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are synthesized from the precursor 2-aminobenzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid via the hydrothermal route. The as-synthesized NCQDs are conjugated with anti-TNT antibodies and characterized using various analytical techniques. The spherical NCQDs are having a size of 5.69 nm. At excitation wavelength 347 nm, the emission wavelength of NCQDs is shifted from 445 nm to 443 nm after their conjugation with anti-TNT Ab. The decrease in photoluminescence emission intensity of NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab with the increased concentration of TNT is regulated by the formation of Jackson-Meisenheimer and antigen–antibody complex, respectively. The detection limit of NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab is observed to be 1.39 X 10-5 M and 1.07 X 10-9 M respectively. The stern–volmer quenching constant of NCQDs is increased from 1.5 X 103 M−1 to 8.5 X 105 M−1 after their conjugation with anti-TNT Ab depicting the elevated affinity of NCQDS/Ab for TNT. The biosensing NCQDs/Ab are found to be more selective towards TNT than the chemosensing NCQDs probe. In spiked real water samples, the RSD of NCQDs/Ab is found to be ± 1.5%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Trinitrotoluene
Antibody
Fluorescent
Biosensing
In-situ

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Anti-TNT antibody conjugated NCQDs (NCQDs/Ab)
TNT detection using NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab
Selectivity of NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab towards TNT
Analysis of TNT in spiked samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Director, AcSIR-CSIO, Chandigarh for his support to perform this study in his premise. Sarita Devi, the first author of the study acknowledges the ICMR-New Delhi. She was granted a research associate fellowship (Grant file No.:45/31/2018-Nan/BMS) as financial support by the ICMR, New Delhi.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The TEM micrograph of as-synthesized NCQDs; [Inset: the size distribution of NCQDs]; b) SAED pattern of NCQDs; [Inset: the calculated d-spacing (in A°) from the SAED rings (up) and the EDS data of NCQDs (down)].
a) Hydrodynamic size of NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab; b) the surface charge on as-synthesized NCQDs at different pH values; c) the RAMAN spectrum of NCQDs; and d) the FTIR spectra of NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab.

a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of NCQDs during various synthesis steps; b) the excitation and PL emission spectra of NCQDs; the PL emission spectra of NCQDs dialyzed for 2.5 hrs (c) and 5 hrs (d) at different excitation wavelengths.

a) The PL emission behavior of EDC/NHS treated NCQDs (NCQDs/EN) (S10.3) with different concentrations anti-TNT Abs; b) Variation in PL emission intensity and λem of NCQDs/EN with increasing concentration of Abs; and PL emission spectra of NCQDs (c) and NCQDs/Ab (d) with an increase in TNT concentrations.

a) The relation between the change in PL intensity of NCQDs in the presence of TNT at different concentrations for LOD determination; b) Q% measurement of NCQDs intensity with TNT presence; c) stern-Volmer plot between the change in PL emission intensity of NCQDs and TNT concentration; and d) Benesi–Hildebrand plot to measure the association constant between the NCQDs and TNT.

head.ce:title: Amino benzene dicarboxylic acid-derived luminescent nitrogen-doped Carbon- quantum Dots/anti-TNT antibodies conjugate for detection of nitroaromatic contaminant in water: A comparative analysis of chemo-Bio-sensing affinity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organization, Chandigarh 160030, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organization, Chandigarh 160030, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Institute of Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University Institute of Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad 201002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Tamil Nadu 600113, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Analytical Techniques Division, CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organization, Chandigarh, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water contaminants are posing deadly impacts on living beings' survival. Nitroaromatic compounds e.g. Trinitrotoluene (TNT), are one among these lethal contaminants and require to be early detected before their consumption. The nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are synthesized from the precursor 2-aminobenzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid via the hydrothermal route. The as-synthesized NCQDs are conjugated with anti-TNT antibodies and characterized using various analytical techniques. The spherical NCQDs are having a size of 5.69 nm. At excitation wavelength 347 nm, the emission wavelength of NCQDs is shifted from 445 nm to 443 nm after their conjugation with anti-TNT Ab. The decrease in photoluminescence emission intensity of NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab with the increased concentration of TNT is regulated by the formation of Jackson-Meisenheimer and antigen–antibody complex, respectively. The detection limit of NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab is observed to be 1.39 X 10-5 M and 1.07 X 10-9 M respectively. The stern–volmer quenching constant of NCQDs is increased from 1.5 X 103 M−1 to 8.5 X 105 M−1 after their conjugation with anti-TNT Ab depicting the elevated affinity of NCQDS/Ab for TNT. The biosensing NCQDs/Ab are found to be more selective towards TNT than the chemosensing NCQDs probe. In spiked real water samples, the RSD of NCQDs/Ab is found to be ± 1.5%.

ce:section.ce:para: Water contaminants are posing deadly impacts on living beings' survival. A large number of nanomaterials have been utilized for treating the water pollutants via different techniques [1] such as Cd/Zr-bimetallic metal–organic frameworks for organic dye pollutants [2], Pt doped Ti/Sb-SnO2 Electrode for phenols [3], Nickel-Nitrogen-Carbon Catalysts for CO2 reduction [4], nanocellulose-based composite materials for water–oil remediation [5], glycine-modified Fe/Zn-layered double hydroxides for arsenic removal [6], and CdSe/gold nanoparticles composites for trinitrotoluene (TNT) [7]. Nitroaromatic compounds e.g. Trinitrotoluene (TNT), are one among these lethal water contaminants and require to be early detected before their consumption [8]. Over the past few decades, owing to the advancement in explosive production at ammunition plants for military purposes and dam or building explosions for civility destruction has generated significant quantities of explosive polluted soil and waterways. The discharge of untreated wastewater has resulted in elevated levels in soil and water. Due to the possible human carcinogenic effects of TNT, highly sensitive and selective TNT detection before its consumption is required [8]. Another major issue with TNT is its possible reduction to aromatic amines, which can be highly mutagenic.

Among the various nanomaterials, carbon-derived nanomaterials are found superior to the rest of all [9–15]. Carbon-quantum dots (CQDs) deliver very fascinating characteristics in photostability, high quantum yield (QY), semiconducting properties, broad-spectrum, size-tunable emission properties, and their chemical coupling due to their colloidal state, etc. [16]. The large surface area to volume ratio and porosity of nanoparticles is another unique characteristic for various applications [17–19]. It renders them suitable for various applications such as biosensing, optoelectronics, photoelectrodes, etc. Heavy metal-based QDs are unsuitable for bioimaging applications due to their cytotoxicity. This drawback can be overcome by using metal-free, highly luminescent organic QDs i.e. carbon derived QDs (CQDs) [20]. This emerging class of CQDs is biocompatible, highly water-soluble, bright photoluminescence (PL), higher photostability, and easy to functionalize due to the presence of edge groups on their surface [21]. These properties widen their scope of applicability to cellular imaging, drug delivery, catalysis, and metal-ion sensing also [22]. The PL spectrum of CQDs can be tuned by their mean size controlled by synthesis parameters such as the precursor to introducing desired functionalities at their graphitic sheet edges [23]. The PL behavior of CQDs is mediated by their quantum size, electron-hole recombination, surface defects, and the triplet ground state of carbene-like free zig-zag sites [24]. Different types of synthesis approach e.g. top-down and bottom-up procedures, lead to the product materials possessing diverse properties rendering it difficult to understand the exact mechanism of their PL [25]. The heteroatom doping can be strategized to tune the surface chemistry of CQDs. The functionalization of CQDs with hetero groups such as amine groups reduces the surface defects and diminishes the carboxyl groups mediated non-radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs. It ends up in the PL enhancement and blue shift [26]. As of now, the surface passivation of CQDs is usually performed for doping wherein their edge groups, like carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, are used as reactive sites [27]. Different passivation reagents have been used such as polyethyleneimine, 1,2-ethylenediamine, branched, or PAMAM-NH2 dendrimer [28–30]. The requirement of extra reagents can be surpassed by using the sole precursor compound with both the hetero group and edge groups without compromising the desired properties of CQDs. 2-aminobenzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid can be explored to achieve the in-situ amine-functionalized CQDs with higher QY, photostability, and PL properties.

Various studies have been reported for the detection of TNT using CQDs via the chemosensing approach due to the formation of the Jackson Meisenheimer (JM) complex between the electron-rich heterogoups on CQDs and the electron-deficient aromatic ring of TNT [31]. The presence of three electron-withdrawing nitro groups on the toluene ring of TNT renders this aromatic ring to be electron-poor enough so an easy loss of a proton from its methyl group. The hydrogen from the methyl is exchanged with the hydrogen from the amine groups of NCQDs. Finally, the lone pair of electrons from the primary amine groups present in NCQDs forms the JM complex with an electron-poor TNT ring [31]. The antibodies are known for their high specificity for target analytes hence, they offer highly sensitive detection. The JM complex-based chemosensing and Ag-Ab complex-based biosensing affinities of single sensing material i.e. CQDs are to be compared in this study. The objective of this paper is to synthesize amine-functionalized CQDs (NCQDs) from the precursor, 2-aminobenzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid for the first time via a bottom-up synthesis approach. These NCQDs are conjugated with anti-TNT antibodies (NCQDs/Ab) for affinity-based biosensing of TNT. The biosensing efficiency of NCQDs/Ab is compared with the chemosensing efficiency of NCQDs. The chemosensing is based on a decrease in the PL intensity of NCQDs in presence of TNT due to the formation of JM complex between the electron-rich amine groups of NCQDs and the electron-deficient aromatic ring of TNT.

The optimization and selection procedure of the final solvent and precursor reagent for CQDs is elaborated in supplementary data (Fig. S2, Fig. S3, and Fig. S4). For this, the precursor concentration and other synthesis parameters were kept constant to monitor the effect of solvent only.

For the synthesis of NCQDs, 10 mg/mL 2-aminobenzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid (ABDC) in ethanol was prepared and vigorously stirred at 1000 rpm/100 °C for 2 min. This solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (TLA) and heated at 180 °C for 12 hrs. After overnight colling at room temperature (RT), the resultant product was monitored for the PL emission property. Thereafter, it was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm/10 min. The 6 mL supernatant was transferred in an activated dialysis membrane and dialyzed for 2.5 hrs against 300 mL DI water with stirring. The PL emission properties of as-prepared NCQDs were also optimized at pH 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 using 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH.

The carboxylic groups present on the NCQDs surface were utilized for their conjugation with anti-TNT antibodies. The NCQDs’ volume was fixed while EDC/NHS concentration was varied maintaining the constant volume. The EDC/NHS molarity was kept as1:2. Therefore, a series of EDC (i.e. 0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1, 5x10-2, 1 x10-2, 5 x10-3, 1 x10-3, 5 x10-4, 4 x10-4 and 5 x10-5 M) and NHS (i.e. 1, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, 02, 0.1, 2x10-2, 1x10-2, 2x10-3, 1x10-3, 2x10-4 and 1x10-4 M) dilutions (5 mL each) was prepared. The NCQDs dilution was mixed with EDC/NHS to achieve NCQDs/EDC/NHS as 1:1:1 v/v. So, a total of 12 NCQDs/EDC/NHS solutions were prepared each containing the 300 μL as-prepared NCQDs and 600 μL of respective molar pair of EDC: NHS::1:2 (300 μL each). Then, these 12 solutions were kept at RT for 30 min and ultra-centrifuged at 4000 rpm/5 min. The precipitate was mixed with 900 μL DI water (precipitate solution: PS). The respective supernatant and PS were denoted as S1 to S12 and PS1 to PS12 respectively. All the solutions were characterized for their UV-absorbance and PL-emission properties. The major change was noticed between solutions 10 and 11. Therefore, intermediate concentration dilutions were also prepared as named 10.1, 10.2, and 10.3 having the EDC/NHS concentrations (in molar) as 4x10-4/8x10-4, 3x10-4/6x10-4, and 2x10-4/4x10-4 respectively, and repeated the same procedure. The optical properties of all the solutions were compared with the reference NCQDs solution (300 μL as-prepared NCQDs + 600 μL DI water).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Anti-TNT antibody conjugated NCQDs (NCQDs/Ab)
ce:section.ce:para: Maintaining the constant volume of EDC/NHS treated NCQDs (S10.3), the antibody concentration was varied in each solution. The 25 μL of anti-TNT Ab dilution was added in 175 μL S10.3. The final volume was set to 1 mL by adding 800 μL of DI water. The anti-TNT Ab stock was prepared as 1 mg/mL in 0.01 M PBS. The Ab dilutions (in 0.01 M PBS) were prepared in a way that the addition of 25 μL of each one would generate the final anti-TNT Ab concentration as 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.2, 6.4 and 12.8 μg/mL in the final solution. The reference S10.3 solution was prepared by adding 175 μL S10.3 and 825 μL DI water. All the S10.3/Ab-dilutions were compared with reference S10.3 solution in terms of their UV-absorbance and PL-emission properties. The synthesis and anti-TNT Ab conjugation procedure of NCQDs are represented in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TNT detection using NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab
ce:section.ce:para: The as-synthesized NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab (with 12.8 μg/mL Ab concentration) were utilized for TNT detection. A stock 1000 ppm TNT was prepared. The TNT dilutions were prepared from this stock in such a way that the addition of 10 μL in 1 mL of NCQDs solution will result in final TNT concentration as 4.4X 10-6, 4.4 X 10-5, 8.8 X 10-5, 13.2 X 10-5, 17.6 X 10-5, 22 X 10-5, 26.4 X 10-5, 30.8 X 10-5, 35.2 X 10-5, 39.6 X 10-5, 4.4 X 10-4, 8.8 X 10-4, 13.2 X 10-4, 17.6 X 10-4 and 22 X 10-4 M. While the final TNT concentration in NCQDs/Ab was 4.4 X 10-8, 2.2 X 10-7, 4.4 X 10-7, 6.6 X 10-7, 8.8 X 10-7, 1.1 X 10-6 and 1.32 X 10-6 M. The PL emission properties of these samples was studied and evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab towards TNT
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity performance of these sensing probes was evaluated against TNT analogs, DNT, DNBA, NB, NP, RDX, and PETN. For this, the same procedure was followed as mentioned above for TNT. The 4.4 X 10-6 M concentration of each analog was assessed for their effect on the PL emission intensity of NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TNT in spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the suspended particles were removed from the tap water using vacuum filtration through a 0.22 mm membrane. The known concentration of as-prepared TNT dilutions from TNT stock solution was subjected to the filtered tap water. Thereafter, the sensing materials, NCQDs, and NCQDs/Ab were added to the TNT spiked tap water samples. The final TNT concentration in the spiked water samples was attained as 10 X 10-8 M, 30 X 10-8 M, and 50 X 10-8 M for both the NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab sensing materials. All these samples were evaluated for the PL emission output to assess the %Recovery and %RSD (n = 3).

To ensure the QY of the as-synthesized NCQDs, the reference dye solution was prepared using Quinine sulfate (QS) in 0.1 M H2SO4. The known QY (Φ) of QS is 54% [32]. The inner filter effect was decreased by adjusting the absorbance of NCQDs and QS solutions < 0.1.

The following Equation was used to determine the QY of NCQDs:
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ
st
(
I x
I
st
)
(
A
st
A x
)
(
È
x
È
st
)
2

where I and A correspond to the fluorescence integral intensity and absorbance, respectively. The refractive index (η) of both the solvents, 0.1 M H2SO4, and water was 1.33. The subscripts ‘x’ and ‘st’ are the sample and standard, QS.

The bright yellow-colored solution of the NCQDs was procured from ABDC via a simple hydrothermal synthesis route at 180 ℃ for 12 hrs. The as-utilized starting material, ABDC is a cheap, easily accessible, and available source for the synthesis of highly luminescent NCQDs. Moreover, the exploitation of ABDC for NCQDs synthesis eliminates the requirement of any kind of external template for their stabilization. It renders this process unique, more economical, and beneficial for practical applications. The morphological and size data as acquired from the TEM measurements at RT is shown in Fig. 1 . As shown in the TEM micrograph (Fig. 1a), the spherical shape and well dispersed NCQDs are formed. The particle-size histogram (inset of Fig. 1a) was attained by statistical analysis of approx. 100 QDs, which demonstrates that the size of CQDs lies in a range from 3.2 to 6.8 nm, with an average diameter of 5.69 nm. Hence, the smaller avg. diameter and the narrow size distribution of NCQDs might be owing to the high pressure and consistent heat energy during hydrothermal treatment.

The diffused rings can be visualized in a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern which points to the poly-nanocrystalline nature of the NCQDs. The calculated interplanar distances were found to be 4.49 A° for NCQDs which is associated with the (002) plane of graphitized carbon. The elemental composition of NCQDs is assessed by EDS measurement as shown in the inset (lower) of Fig. 1b. The presence of nitrogen in EDS data confirmed the successful synthesis of amine-functionalized CQDs from ABDC.

The optical properties of as-synthesized NCQDs are studied from the DLS zeta sizer, RAMAN, FTIR, UV–visible, and PL emission spectrophotometers. The hydrodynamic diameter of as-synthesized NCQDs was observed to be 24.57 nm (Fig. 2 a). The size difference in TEM and DLS data can be credited to the fact that the most compact state of particles is studied by TEM whereas DLS is followed in a solvated state. Different non-covalent interactions (e.g. H-bonding, van-der Waals interactions, π-πstacking, etc.) are possible between the edge groups (–COOH, –OH), amine groups of NCQDs, and the solvent molecules. These surface functionalities are involved in the formation of the outermost solvation layer with the solvent resulting in the large particle size data.

At different pH values, the surface zeta potential of NCQDs was also monitored by DLS (Fig. 2b) to validate its stability and applicability for different applications. It can be observed that the as-synthesized NCQDs possess a positive surface charge (+38 mV) which confirms their higher dispersibility in water and stability. At pH ranging from 2 to 6, the NCQDs bear a high positive charge showing excellent stability at this pH range. Although, beyond this pH range (>pH 6), there was a noticeable decrement in their surface charge and so the dispersibility. It suggests that numerous amino ion groups are present on NCQDs' surfaces. The oxygen-rich and cationic functionalities improved the hydrophilicity and stability in an aqueous medium, manifesting these NCQDs for sensing application in aqueous systems.

The defects in the NCQDs were analyzed by RAMAN spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). In the RAMAN spectrum, two prominent peaks named the D-band at 1357 cm−1 and the G-band at 1589 cm−1 were observed. These are commonly attributed to the disordered D and crystalline G characteristics of the NCQDs respectively [33]. The ID/IG i.e. intensity ratio of the D-band and G-band was found as 0.92, indicating a large portion of crystalline carbon in the NCQDs structure. This data confirms the formation of NCQDs in the solution.

The presence of specific bonds was recognized by FTIR spectroscopy using vibrational states in the chemical structure of the materials and illustrated in Fig. 2d and S5. The broad and strong peak present at 3442 cm−1 is due to the intermolecular hydrogen bond O–H stretching vibrations and N–H stretching vibrations of primary amines in CQDs (Fig. S5). The medium peaks in the range 3000–2840 cm−1 are related to the C–H stretching vibrations. In Fig. 2d, the absorption peak located at 1631 cm−1 is assigned to the C = C stretching of cyclic alkene in NCQDs. The medium intensity peaks at 1422 cm−1 can be assigned to the O–H bending vibration of the carboxylic acid in NCQDs. The medium absorption peak located at 1383 cm−1 and 1121 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–N of amines in NCQDs [33]. The peaks at 802 cm−1 and 874 cm−1 are derived from the di- or tri-substituted C–H bending vibrations. Therefore, the FTIR data demonstrate the presence of hydroxyl, carboxylic, and N-containing groups in the structure of as-synthesized NCQDs from ABDC. It could be due to the hydrothermal treatment which tends to facilitate the ABDC subjection to dehydration, decomposition, polymerization, and carbonization resulting in numerous carbon skeletons.

The light absorption and emission properties of as-synthesized NCQDs were studied through UV–visible and PL emission spectrophotometry as shown in Fig. 3 . Initially, the effect of dialysis procedure and dialysis time was finalized from the absorption spectral study of NCQDs (Fig. 3a). The NCQDs after centrifugation and before dialysis was observed to show a broad absorption in the wavelength range from 250 nm to 440 nm. It can be possibly due to the presence of various unreacted byproducts or impurities left even after centrifugation. The presence of different-sized NCQDs might be another reason for this absorption behavior.

After being dialyzed for 5 hrs, the absorbance was decreased drastically which can be credited to the excessively crossing impurities or ions from the solution in the NCQDs present in the dialysate. It could be hindering the absorption of the main material. But, the presence of a rather low-intensity absorbance fingerprint of NCQDs denotes the presence of monodispersed and uniformly sized NCQDs in the dialysate. Therefore, it can be stated that the dialysis time should be lowered from 5 hrs. The next dialysis was performed for 2.5 hrs and observed for the absorbance spectrum of dialysate. Very refined and resolved absorption peaks can be observed in the absorption spectra of NCQDs dialyzed for 2.5 hrs. Hence, the dialysis time of 2.5 hrs was finalized for the synthesis of ABDC-derived NCQDS. The absorption peak in the ultraviolet region corresponds to the absorption of the aromatic-π system which designates the successful synthesis of CQDs from ABDC [34]. The absorbance maximum at wavelength, λmax 225 nm is assigned to the π- π* transition of the π-orbitals of aromatic carbon C = C bond. This absorption behavior in the lower UV region is caused by polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAHs) having large energy band gaps such as two or three-ringed PAH [35]. The absorbance peak at wavelength 258 nm indicates the n– π* transition of carbonyl (C = O) groups of CQDs. The specific absorbance peak at 345 nm shows the presence of n– π* transition of C-N bond in NCQDs which confirms the formation of amine-functionalized NCQDs from ABDC. The unshared/lone pair of electrons containing the amine group, an auxochrome, lead to the absorption at a higher wavelength i.e. 345 nm. This is the NCQDs structural derived absorbance phenomenon. Under the UV-illumination, these NCQDs exhibited an intense blue-colored emission.

The PL emission spectrum of NCQDs at an excitation wavelength of 347 nm is shown in Fig. 3b. At λex 347 nm, the NCQDs show the highest emission at λem 445 nm. The PL emission property of NCQDs as dialyzed for 2.5 hrs and 5 hrs are shown in Fig. 3c and 3d respectively. At excitation wavelengths ranging from 330 nm to 370 nm (with 10 nm interval), the NCQDs (2.5 hrs) delivered the high-intensity emission at λem 445 nm (λex 350 nm). Conversely, the NCQDs (5 hrs) did not provide a noteworthy emission. Even though, their highest emission was also observed at λem 445 nm (λex 350 nm). The PL emission data of NCQDs prepared at different dialysis periods is also found in complete agreement with the absorbance properties. It can be stated that the dialysis time should be fixed at 2.5 hrs to achieve the highly luminescent NCQDs from ABDC. The effect of pH, time UV light irradiation, and ionic strength of solvent on NCQDs PL property is shown in Fig. S6.

For the conjugation of NCQDs with anti-TNT Abs, the NCQDs were initially subjected to EDC/NHS treatment. The EDC/NHS/NCQDs best concentration ratio was optimized to eliminate the unwanted diminishing effect of EDC/NHS on NCQDs Pl emission properties as shown in Fig. S7 and S8.
The hydrodynamic size of NCQDs was found to be increased from 24.57 d.nm to 107.09 d.nm.

(Fig. 2a). The possible reason is the presence of a polymeric molecule and antibodies on the surface of NCQDs that lead to the large increment in the overall hydrodynamic size of NCQDs/Ab. The FTIR spectra of NCQDs/Ab exhibited several additional peaks than those of NCQDs (Fig. 2d). Specifically, the absorption peaks at 1631–1600 cm−1 are likely related to the C = C and C = O stretching of α,β-unsaturated ketone. The intensified absorption peak at 1383 cm−1 is ascribed to the increased C = C and C-N stretching vibrations due to the presence of antibodies on the surface of NCQDs. The broad absorption peaks located at 3416 cm−1 (Fig. S5) and 1316–1269 cm−1 are attributed to the C-N stretching vibrations in aromatic amines of NCQDs/Ab. The absorption peaks at 1197–1091 cm−1 are due to the C-O and C-N stretching vibrations of tertiary alcohol and C-N of amines respectively. The FTIR data confirmed the successful conjugation of NCQDs with anti-TNT Abs.

The PL emission behavior of S10.3 (or NCQDs/EN) is monitored with an increase in anti-TNT Abs concentration ranging from 0.025 μg/mL to 12.8 μg/mL Abs in the final solution (Fig. 4 a). It can be discerned that at high Abs concentration the PL emission intensity was achieved back while at lower Abs concentration, there was a decrease in the PL emission intensity of NCQDs/EN S10.3. From Fig. 4b, it can be perceived that at lower Ab concentration there was a red shift in λem from 442 nm to 447 nm, which was realigned back to 443 nm at higher Ab concentration. Beyond 3.2 μg/mL, anti-TNT Ab concentration, there was saturation in the emission wavelength shift. The possible reason behind this behavior can be the dilution factor at lower Abs concentration resulting in a sharp, instant decrease in the PL emission intensity and the redshift in emission wavelength. While at higher Abs concentration, the Abs present in the sample exceed the dilution factor and the PL intensity was attained back. Therefore, further experimentation for TNT detection was carried forward with NCQDs/ 12.8 μg/mL Abs.

Both the materials i.e. NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab were inspected and compared for their TNT chemosensing and biosensing efficiency. The schematic illustration of the TNT sensing study using the respective materials is shown in Fig. S9.

For TNT detection using the NCQDs as sensing material, the PL emission spectra of as-prepared NCQDs were monitored in the presence of increasing TNT concentration. The TNT concentration was varied from 4.4X 10-6 M to 2.2 X 10-3 M as shown in Fig. 4c. It can be spotted that with the increase in TNT concentration, there is a successive decrease in PL emission intensity of NCQDs (λex 347 nm, λem 445 nm). The theoretical interpretation of the TNT detection capability of NCQDs in terms of detection limit (LOD), percentage quenching (Q%), Stern–Volmer constant (KSV), and the association constant (Ka) of CQDs and TNT by Benesi–Hildebrand method is performed and illustrated in Fig. 5 a, b, c, and d respectively. The Q% in the NCQDs emission intensity was demonstrated using the following relation [36]:
2
Q % =
I 0
- I
I 0
× 100

Where I0 and I are the PL emission intensity of NCQDs in the absence and the presence of TNT. The resultant Q% was found to be proportional to the TNT concentration. More than 75% quenching was observed at 1.6 X 10-3 M and 2.2 X 10-3 M TNT concentration in NCQDs solution (Fig. 5b). The relation between the change in PL emission intensity (I0-I) at λem 445 nm was plotted against the increase in TNT concentration (in M) in NCQDs to determine the detection limit (LOD) of this NCQDs based chemosensing platform (Fig. 5a). The LOD was calculated using the following equation [37]:
3
LOD =
k . S D
b

Where k = 3; SD denotes the standard deviation in the variation in PL emission intensity of the blank at 445 nm; ‘b’ depicts the slope of the regression line in the plot I0-I vs [TNT] in M. A definite linear decrease in NCQDs emission intensity was witnessed in the presence of TNT.

The linear regression relation of the plot I0-I vs [TNT] ranging from 4.4 X 10-6 to 4.4 X 10-4 M (R2 = 0.996) as evidenced in Fig. 5a, can be presented as:
4
Δ I =
I 0
- I = 7.29 x 10
5
C
here C represents the TNT concentration in M.

While from 4.4 X 10-4 to 2.2 X 10-3 M TNT (R2 = 0.985), the rate of change in PL intensity differed as:
5
Δ I =
I 0
- I = 1.26 x 10
5
C

Therefore, this NCQDs-based chemosensing nanoprobe delivered a very low LOD value of about 1.39 X 10-5 M. Under the optimized parameters, the TNT mediated quenching in the NCQDs PL emission is perfectly explicit by the stern-Volmer relation (Fig. 5c) as represented here [37]:
6
I 0
I
=
K
sv
Q
+ C

In this equation, the I0 and I stand for initial and final PL emission intensity of NCQDs in the absence and presence of analyte TNT respectively; ‘Ksv’ denotes the stern-Volmer constant which represents the affinity between the sensing material, NCQDs, and the target analyte molecule, TNT (i.e. donor/acceptor affinity); ‘Q’ represents the TNT concentration, and ‘C’ is a constant (C=∼1). The linear regression equation (R2 = 0.99) for the change in PL emission of NCQDs against TNT concentration can be represented as:
7
I 0
I
- 1 = 1.52 x
10
3
Q

The Ksv is found to be 1.52 X 103 M−1 in the concentration range of 4.4 X 10-6 M to 17.6 X 10-4 M (Fig. 5c). The association constant of the NCQDs with TNT is found by Benesi–Hildebrand equation (Fig. 5d) by following the equation [38]:
8
1
I 0
- I
=
1
I 0
-
I b
+
1
k a
I 0
-
I b
[ T N T ]

where Ka represents the apparent association constant; I0 and Ib stand for the PL emission intensity of NCQDs in the absence and presence of TNT excess, and ‘I’ represents the observed PL emission intensity of NCQDs in the presence of different TNT concentrations. The 1/(I0-I) value is plotted with 1/[TNT] and the intercept/slope value was measured to find the Ka. The final association constant is obtained as 2 X 103 M−1 which depicts the high sensitivity of NCQDs for TNT.

Aligning to the principle of biosensing based on the formation of antigen–antibody complex, the TNT detection efficiency of anti-TNT Abs conjugated NCQDs-based immunosensing probe was explored. Sustaining the same, it can be perceived from the Fig. 4d, that the PL emission intensity of NCQDs/Abs is found to decrease sequentially with the increase in TNT concentration from 4.4 X 10-8 M to 1.32 X 10-6 M at λex 347 nm, λem 443 nm. Consequently, the increased Q% in the PL emission intensity of NCQDs/Abs (at λex 347 nm) with increased TNT amount was also measured using equation (2) and is delivered in Fig. S9a. The net Q% is observed to be increased from about 7.8% at 4.4 X 10-8 M TNT to 80 % at 1.32 X 10-6 M TNT. Superior to the TNT mediated quenching in NCQDs emission of about 75% at 1.6 X 10-3 M TNT, here about 80% quenching is reported even at the lower TNT concentration, 1.32 X 10-6 M. It can be credited to the high specificity of the bioreceptor molecules, anti-TNT antibodies for its analyte. Furthermore, the LOD of NCQDs/Ab immunoprobe respecting TNT is also assessed using the relation between I0- I at λex 347 nm, and λem 443 nm (Fig. S9b). Here, the I0 and I are the initial and final PL emission intensities of NCQDs/Ab in the absence and the presence of TNT. A sharp linear decrease in NCQDs/Ab intensity is witnessed in Fig. S9b. The LOD of the NCQDs/Ab probe is also measured using the above-mentioned equation (3) and found to be 1.07 X 10-9 M. Their linear regression equation for the LOD value of the NCQDs/Ab/TNT system can be illustrated as (R2 = 0.988):
9
Δ I =
I 0
- I = 5.54 x 10
8
C
where ‘C’ represents the TNT concentration (M). Therefore, it can be stated that the NCQDs-based biosensing probe is much more highly sensitive than that of the NCQDs-based chemosensing nanoprobe.

The Ksv and Ka of NCQDs/Ab in the presence of TNT at λex 347 nm and λem 443 nm are also measured and shown in Fig. S9c and S9d, respectively. The Ksv is measured using the above mention equation (6) and the linear regression relation can be presented thereof as (R2 = 0.99):
10
I 0
I
- 1 = 8.6 x
10
5
Q

here the I0 and I are also the initial and final PL emission intensities of NCQDs/Ab in the absence and the presence of TNT. The resultant Ksv was found to be 8.65 X 105 M−1 which is very high (hundred times) than that of the NCQDs/TNT system. It depicts the higher affinity of NCQDs/Ab probe for TNT than that of NCQDs alone. Subsequently, the Ka value for NCQDs/AB/TNT system at λex 347 nm is calculated using the relation mentioned in equation (8) (R2 = 0.977) and found as 2.3 X 106 M−1 (Fig. S9d). This increased value of Ka for the NCQDs/AB/TNT system (thousand times) than that of the NCQDs/TNT chemosensing system surely implicates the higher sensitivity of this immunosensing probe towards TNT. The principle behind the chemosensing and biosensing of TNT using the NCQDs and NCQDs/Ab sensing probes is illustrated in Fig. S9 and explained. The selectivity study is presented in Fig. S10, S11, and Fig. S12. The recovery data of spiked and the real water sample (n = 3) is presented in Table S3. The comparison of the actual sample detection by this developed method and some of the important standard methods is shown in Table S4.

The present research work conveys the output that the NCQDs can be utilized for TNT detection based on JM complex formation to achieve good LOD as well as selectivity performance. Although these NCQDs outstand as immunosensing probes in conjugation with anti-TNT Abs (NCQDs/Ab) in context to both the parameters i.e. sensitivity and selectivity. As it is proved from the study that these ABDC-derivatized NCQDs possess excellent optical properties, photostability, high luminescence (better QY ∼ 53.4% than other reported CQDs), easy and rapid synthesis procedure, and above all, without the requirement of any surface passivating reagent or post-synthetic treatment for amine functionalization. It renders these NCQDs superior to the CQDs mentioned in other reported methods. Therefore, we present the ABDC-derived NCQDs-based highly sensitive and selective chemo/biosensing probe for TNT in water.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Director, AcSIR-CSIO, Chandigarh for his support to perform this study in his premise. Sarita Devi, the first author of the study acknowledges the ICMR-New Delhi. She was granted a research associate fellowship (Grant file No.:45/31/2018-Nan/BMS) as financial support by the ICMR, New Delhi.
